content.claim: Between 2010 and 2020, students identified with special educational needs in Hungary rose from 6.7% to 7.7% in primary education and from 3.9% to 6.2% in secondary education. The drivers are contested: partly attributed to greater diagnostic awareness and improved diagnostics, and partly to social disparities and workforce shortages.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Demography lens
limitations: Source does not quantitatively decompose 'real prevalence change' from 'diagnostic practice change'
Cannot distinguish whether the 1-2 percentage point rise reflects increased underlying conditions, improved detection, or relabeling of existing students
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
